---
title: Pacing in Interactive Fiction
tags: [pacing, structure]
---

# Pacing in Interactive Fiction

Pacing in a branching story is not a single throughline the author can
smooth by hand — every path a reader might take needs its own rhythm.
A commit beat that lands too soon after the last one reads as frantic;
one that lands too late reads as drift. Distribute the big decisions
across the length of a path rather than clustering them near the
climax, and let quiet beats breathe between them.

## Commit spacing

Space dilemma commitments so a reader never faces two irreversible
choices back to back without at least one scene to metabolize the
first. Clustering commits is the most common pacing failure in
branching drafts: it reads as a checklist of choices, not a story.

## Quiet beats

A quiet beat — no decision, no reveal — is not wasted space. It gives
the previous choice room to matter before the next one arrives, and it
is where voice and atmosphere do their heaviest lifting.
